options: The first commit in a repository
The default name for your remote repository, usually the copy on GitHub
The branch you are currently working on
A file that lists ignored paths
questions.explanation: Origin is the default name Git gives your remote repository. Your local repo and origin are separate copies you sync with push and pull.

options: Downloads commits from the remote into your branch
Uploads your local commits to the remote repository
Creates a new branch on the remote
Deletes the remote copy of your work
questions.explanation: Push sends your local commits up to the remote so others can see them. Pull is the opposite: it brings remote commits down to you.

questions.prompt: How does git fetch differ from git pull?
options: Fetch uploads commits; pull downloads them
Fetch downloads remote commits without merging; pull downloads and merges in one step
They are identical commands with different names
Fetch only works on the main branch
questions.explanation: Fetch downloads remote commits so you can inspect them but does not change your working branch. Pull is fetch plus merge, updating your branch immediately.

questions.prompt: Why do testers value pull requests?
options: They make tests run automatically without any setup
They show a diff for review and can gate the merge on approval and passing checks
They replace the need for branches entirely
They commit code faster than git commit
questions.explanation: A pull request shows every change as a reviewable diff and blocks merging until reviewers approve and checks pass, turning 'trust me' into a gated, reviewed record.
